description: Apollo 10 Flight Journal - Day 3, part 11: TV and a long, long rest

tokens: We are standing by for a TV feed now.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Hello, Apollo 10.
Houston.
The networks and the Goldstone is all configured, you can turn on the tube.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Apollo 10 is approaching 175,000 miles [324,000 km] as it prepares for this television transmission.
We are showing 174,754 nautical miles [323,643 km].
Velocity; 3,406 feet per second [1,038 m/s].
We'll stand by for the TV which should be coming up shortly.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Okay.
We will get it out here and play it.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Hello, Houston.
This is Apollo 10.
You ought to be receiving something now.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Stand by.
It is not coming in here yet.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay.
We are just starting.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Roger.
Will it be exterior shots, Gene-o?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Negative.
We'll just start interior right away and then take you outside.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: And then we'll bring you back inside.
But we will start inside, take you outside, and bring you inside for the water bag.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay.
Let me know when you are receiving us.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Roger.
We will.
The networks and all are configured for this, so we are standing by.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Let us know when you are getting a "pic," Charlie.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Roger.
Sure will.
EECOMs are saying we got a 90-second warm up on that transmitter, so it might take just a little bit longer.
Okay.
We got the black and white coming in now.
The black and white just came in.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Let us know when...
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Let us know when you get color.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay.
We are seeing your patch now in black and white.
Be just a few more seconds.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Color coming now.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Okay.
We just got the color, 10, on the vidicon here and it is looking real good, maybe a little bit focus; but the colors are good and it is a nice, simple little patch we see.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: This is the peacock of Apollo 10.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: And we'd like to say hello from the five of us, if we may.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Roger.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay.
You want me to be a straight man on that question and ask it?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Stand by one.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Negative.
Stand by one.
Got a little technical difficulties here.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We are still getting the color, 10.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay, 10.
Here's the hello.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay.
Go ahead.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Here's hello from the five of us from on Apollo 10.
Here's Tom Stafford.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: He's a beautiful Tom Stafford there.
He's in living color.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: John Young.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We've got John.
He's a little dark down there now, with his lights not on him, but we can tell it's John with his chin strap loose.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: And yours truly, Gene Cernan.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Roger.
We got you, Gene.
The sun is up pretty bright.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Are you still with us?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Yes sir.
The Sun is pretty bright coming back out - Now you are coming in better.
We see you slipping down in the LEB.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay.
That's the three of us.
Here's the other two on Apollo 10, your friendly Charlie Brown and our ever loving companion, Snoopy.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Roger.
We got it coming in now.
Okay.
Color on Charlie Brown and Snoopy is a little dark.
If you could get a little bit more light on them, it would be fine, but we can recognize the characters.
They look pretty happy up there.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: How's that?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: That's fine.
It looks a little dark on the color.
Could you stop it open a little bit more?
Wait a minute.
Okay.
That's fine now.
There you go.
The red and the background on the cards are coming in fine.
We are washing out a little bit on the white of Charlie Brown's coat and Snoop's face.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay.
Now, you know that there are five of us up here.
We'd like to take you outside and show you what the five of us are looking at.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Roger.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We got the big Earth in color and it looks like about a half-Earth to us.
It's a beautiful blue.
We see the tremendous cloud coverage that you were talking about throughout the day, 10.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay, Charlie.
you are looking at the world rightsideup, as we know it.
The Gulf of Mexico - Mexico goes down and to the right of the picture toward the terminator, South America is in the lower right, and corner of the picture on the terminator.
You can look up right smack in the center of the whole picture.
You can make out Mexico, is Houston, right on the Gulf, and then North America goes up to about the 11 o'clock position on your picture.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Roger.
We copy.
We see primarily just the blues of the ocean and the whites of the clouds.
We have a - the cloud patterns are pretty evident.
Agree quite - real closely with the weather map I have.
It is pretty difficult to pick out the landmasses, though, I must admit.
We see one brownish area which appears to be in the American desert, about the center of the globe right now.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Yes, Charlie.
That's Mexico and the southwestern United States, right there; and Baja California is on the left of that, and the right-hand edge happens to be the Gulf of Mexico.
If you can follow it all up, you will follow it right to Houston and thence New Orleans.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: It's awfully hard to ascertain the difference - Okay, Charlie.
It's hard to ascertain between the water down there in the Gulf and the landmass, because the whole eastern coast of the United States looks a greenish brown versus water.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Roger.
That helps us out here to locate ourselves, at least for me, 10; and I think I see where you are talking about now.

We have one section of clouds that looks like it is almost a circular area - a clear area and then clouds appear to come out of South and Central America - swing out into the Pacific and in the center of that it looks like the clear area which I am saying is the southern part of the United States, from Mexico along the Gulf Coast.
Is that correct?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: That's it, Charlie.
The Gulf of Mexico is right smack in the center up and down of the world.

If you follow the terminator down and went halfway and then went about halfway from there toward the rounder part of the Earth, you will find the Gulf of Mexico on that brown area you are looking at, between Mexico and the southeastern United States.
That's Houston right smack in the center of that clear area.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Roger.
It appears...
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: That clear area goes from Central America right on up into the states.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Roger.
We copy.
It appears that the landmasses are washing out just about as much as the clouds.
Could you open it up a couple of stops and then stop it down fast so we can get a little bit - a second or two of sharper definition?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay.
Let us know when it is a little better.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay.
It was a little - there you go.
If you can hold that, but I think - That's good right there.
It is a lot better, 10.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Charlie, we are full zoom on you and it's even hard for us to make out things with the naked eye unless we know where they are.
So, I imagine it is going to be difficult for you.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay, Charlie.
The total globe that you see there is bigger than what we actually see, since we have the zoom lens on and it is probably about one and one-quarter times as big as we see it.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Copy, 10.

As I said earlier, we are primarily getting the globe on a black background, and we see the white of the clouds and the blues of the sea with an occasional glimpse of what I make out as landmasses in the brownish.
But, it is really difficult for an untrained eye to pick out the exact landmasses.
We are sitting here with the in-pad book that - We got it to show the various sizes and with this diagram, it is a big help.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: What you are saying, Charlie, is that we are too far away to give you a good picture.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Boy, you sure are a long way away.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I think what it is, 10, is the landmasses and the clouds tend to wash out and it is a little hard to discern the difference; but looks like we can pick out the shapes of Yucatan, Florida, and Cuba, and the Gulf of Mexico.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Charlie, let me ask.
Do you see the area you said was a clear area, and do you think you could pick up Mexico there?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay.

If you follow up - but you might think it is the Gulf of Mexico there - and then go straight north you see a little bit of V in the clouds and there's one going off to the right and a little thin sliver going to the right is the one I've been mentioning all day that goes from Indiana on through the northeast part of the country; and then that bigger blob that forms the left-hand side of the V is over the north, central United States and then right smack in center of the V is Lake Superior and Lake Michigan.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Roger.
That is a good description, 10.
It clears it up for me, anyway.
I can see what you are talking about, now.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: And then way up on the upper left-hand, maybe about 10 o'clock on the globe, you will see a funny cloud pattern that sort of looks like a sea serpent of some sort with his beak pointing to the right.
That that cloud pattern that Tom was referring to up in the Alaska area.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Roger.
That's nearly apparent to us.
When you stop it down, we can see that pattern.
Some of the time, though, it's washed out due to the tremendous cloud coverage in that area.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: There it is, Charlie.
That ought to be good.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay.
It just came in on the black and white.
We'll see it in just a second.
Okay, now we see what you're talking about.
Looks like an inverted view, almost.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Now you ought to be - Right, now you ought to be able to see that V-area I was talking about better, too.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Roger.
It's coming in a lot better, Gene.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay.
If you got a pretty good view of the outside, we'll take you back inside for one last quick minute.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Thanks a lot, 10, for that view.
It's real good.
We'll be standing by for the water-bag trick.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay.
We'll take you back inside here, now.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: I'll take care of it.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Question for today: who is that?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: It looks like John Young with four sets of eyes, there.
Two sets of eyes.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: There you go.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Let's ball it four eyes.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Looks like a World War I aviator.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: That's what happens when you look through the telescope to see the Sun.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Okay.
It may sound like we've been loafing for the past couple of days, but we haven't.
We've been real busy, and every spare minute we get we study our Flight Plan.

So you see that pretty soon we're going to be going into orbit, and we have a completely different set of operations to go into that shows our pitch profile all around the Moon, for the first revolution.
Tomorrow's a big day, and we're very much looking for it.
Even though we're about 180,000 miles [333,000 km] away from the Earth here, you never get away from studying.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: That's a great picture, 10.
We can see the various spacecraft attitudes with the dark side of the Moon and the bright side.
And we see the LM and the Command Module linked together and going into a LOI-1 burn attitude.
It's real clear.
You can almost read the writing on the pages.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Roger.
Don't adjust your set.
It's in black and white.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Roger.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We can read on the...
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: If you want to know where we're going - We'll show you a little bit of a chart of where we're going.
Day after tomorrow, we ought to be seeing this in living Moon color.
Right now, it's in the best black and white we've got.
This is the area around Landing Site 2.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Roger.
Could you open it up a little bit, 10?
We think maybe if you get a little bit wider f-stop, it'll help us out.
Your pictures are a little bit dark.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: That's a lot better, 10.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I can't see the f-stop, yet.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Okay, 10.
When Gene moved back, it helped out a lot.
If you'll just pull the chart back - that's good now.
We can - It's coming in a lot better.
Gene, could you...
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: The moral of the story is, John - The moral of the story is, John just said, "You know you can study all your life and you never finish studying," and here we are almost a quarter of a million miles away, and we're still studying.
Like Tom said, he's got two girls taking final exams this week, and this is his way of saying, "Get to work."
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Yes, and if those girls are listening, they had better be studying tonight.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Okay.
I'm sure they're listening.
Would - Gene, how about pointing to a Landing Site 2 on the map for the folks?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay.
Landing Site 2, I'll show you here in just a second.
We'll be coming up from the bottom and I'll stop my finger up on Landing Site 2.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: That's the area we hope to bring back some good pictures of in a couple of days.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We'll eventually - That's the area where we'll eventually be actually landing on the surface of the Moon.
This area is probably just about visible from - in the lighted - with the terminator of the Moon lighted, I mean, from the Earth at this time.
just about.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Roger.
We copy.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: That's one advantage we have.
If you don't like to turn your pages, you can always turn yourself instead.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Commander, you're in rare form today.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We figure that, you know, there's always a way of making pigs run downhill and maybe even knowledge will make it run that way.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Well, I guess that's the message to the kiddies in the country.
If they can't get their homework right-side-up, go upside-down.
They might be able to absorb more that way.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Roger.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Looks like John's trying to hog the picture there, Gene.
Why don't you - there you go, you pushed him out of the - you got - You got center stage now.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: What a ham.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: You want to see me push; watch what happens.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: That's called one-finger power.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Roger.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay, this is Apollo 10 signing off.
We'll give you one more picture of the Earth here, and call it a day.
Oh, hold it.
We want to show you the bag, too.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Roger.
We'd appreciate that.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: The bag is full of - half, full of bubbly water.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: And for those of you who are unfamiliar, there's the valve where the air -and then the water comes out, and here's the handle.
And notice, they're both on the same side.
Are you ready?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Go ahead.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: It's pretty difficult - get some more light on it, 10.
It's pretty difficult to see the bubbles from that position.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I'll show you the bubbles, after I stop.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay.
It was full of the [garble] thousands of minute little bubbles.
You wouldn't pick them up.
You can barely see them with the naked eye, but they're there.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: l0, try spinning a little bit harder, and maybe that'll put the bubbles to the top.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Man, I spinned it so hard a little while ago, I was going in the other direction (laughter).
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Roger.
Gene.
Hold it up next to the LEB DSKY.
We couldn't see any bubble there.
If you got one together.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Hey, that's a good spin; you're really spinning it now.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay.
How's that for a bubble now?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay.
We got it.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay.
We see that bubble.
Really a big one.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: And it's in the bottom.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Charlie, about the only thing we get by spinning them is making the little bubbles into a big one and then it stays in the bottom.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Roger.
Is there any water left in the top of the bag, 10?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: No, there's no water in the top of the bag.
All the water's out.
We have tried it with semi-filled bags, with full bags, with half-full bags.
What we really have been able to do, I think, is to get the bubble concentrated and then suck the water out from around it.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Roger.
Have you tried it filling up - filling the bag - both top and the bottom of the bag and then spinning it?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Yes.
We tried that, too.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Well, it doesn't look like it works then, does it?
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Charlie, you can see the bubble real good, can't you?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Roger.
We have it, Tom.
It's quite evident here to us.
We'll have the experts look at this and maybe they can come up with something for later on this evening for you.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Hey, Charlie, it is true, though, that water goes to the bottom of the bag.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: And that phenomenon we have proved.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Also, when the air gets down there, we can stop spinning; the big bubble is at the bottom.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Roger.
We copy.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay.
This is Apollo 10.
We'll take you outside for one last look at the Earth and sign off.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Roger.
Thank you very much.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay, 10.
We just got the exterior view, and we got the Earth in the center of the screen, and it's a little bit different orientation this time.
We see the North Pole up in the northeast about the 2 o'clock position on our screen now.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: And he'll rotate the camera over a little bit.
He was just tilting it for ease of handling here.
He's rotated it back now, Charlie.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Roger.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: And from the five of us on Apollo 10: Tom Stafford, John Young, Eugene Cernan, Charlie Brown and Snoopy; we hope you've enjoyed it today.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Thank you much, 10.
We appreciated the show; it was very nice.
We'll see you tomorrow.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay.
And tomorrow we should be around the Moon.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: 10, Houston.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Go ahead.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Roger, Tom.

On this water bag, the only thing we can suggest is fill the bag up completely full and then spin; and then if you got - need more water, fill it up again and then spin it and try until it's completely full, and then maybe slowly kneading that bubble up to the top.

And if that doesn't work, then our only suggestion is going to the fruit juice bag and/or food bag and filling it up and then spinning it until you get a big bubble, and then kneading it up to the top where the food port is and evacuating it that way.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Roger.
We've tried most of that but we'll press on here.
And again, we're all thinking here, if that's the only problem we've got on this mission, we're going to be in great shape.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Roger.
We concur.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Yes, I mean.
You can tell what kind of shape we're in when we can talk about things like that.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Roger.
Hey, it...
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: There is one thing I want Charlie to check.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Go ahead, 10.
It appears just...
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: You sure like to talk a lot, Charlie.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I'm sorry.
This time delay I think is giving us some problem.
If you just keep talking, and when you hear me you're still downlinking and I'm still receiving so if I interrupt you, I'll just stop talking if you start or when I'm talking.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay.
Real good, Charlie.
We'll try to be more observant on that.
And again, you might give us a time when you want us to start the PTC mode again, and also I just want to check, is the color still looking pretty good on the TV?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Stafford is keen to return the spacecraft to the PTC mode to ensure they don't encounter any thermal issues from staying too long in one attitude.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Roger.
We thought it was real good here, 10.
The Earth - the interior - Hold on.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Back with you, l0.
The interior shots in some of the darker portions of the spacecraft looked a little dull.
However, when you're in the floodlights, everything was real fine.
The exterior was very good, we thought.
The whites and the blues and the Earth looked fine.
We think the colors are real good.
Everybody's real pleased with the operation of the camera.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay.

Real good, and the main thing too, I'm hoping that from the resolution that we have on the device, that when we get around the Moon tomorrow, we should show you some real good terrain features with the resolution we have on the instrument.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Roger.
We're looking forward to that.
We think we'll be in pretty good shape.
And we'll come up with a PTC time for you momentarily.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Charlie, were the pictures that we shot over the Straits of Gibraltar and the ones where we picked up South and North America over the whole Atlantic through Madrid, did they get played back to you?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We haven't seen them yet.
The ones from Madrid will take 30 hours for us to get those.
The Goldstone we're going to play back shortly, 10.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay.
I guess we're at about the distance where the resolution on the camera doesn't really give you a chance to look at the Earth too closely.
So, I guess we'll probably wait till we get on back to get any good Earth pictures.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Roger.
The colors are still brilliant, but the resolution is fairly marginal now.
You really have to have a map in front of you to pick out what you're describing.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Hello, Apollo 10.
Houston.
You can initiate PTC at your convenience with the same procedure as utilized last night.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: See the commentary on the PTC initiation at026:55:33.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Okay.
We're going to go ahead now and start to pick it up.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Hey...
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 54 hours, 8 minutes.
That TV transmission that was recorded at Goldstone several hours ago, will be transmitted to Houston and released at 6:00 pm Central Daylight Time about 2 minutes from now.
Duration is 4 minutes, 47 seconds.
While we are feeding that playback of the television, we will record any live audio transmissions from the spacecraft and play those back after the television transmission.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Apollo 10, Houston.
We'd like you to do a Verb 45.
We saw you when you loaded the DAP - that 44 accidentally which set the surface flag -- and we'd like to reset that bit.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Duke is pointing out an error in the procedure for setting up the Digital Auto Pilot.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Yes.
We just caught it, and we're getting out the G&N dictionary to do it ourselves.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Roger.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I believe we're one mission too soon for that one.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Long comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 54 hours, 16 minutes.
Apollo 10 is 176,221 miles [326,359 km] from Earth.
Its velocity is 3,377 feet per second [1,029 m/s].
We're getting ready for a change of shift here in the control center.
Shifts will change at 6:30 Central Daylight Time.
We're estimating the change of shift news briefing for 7:00 pm.
We have about 40 seconds worth of Cape accumulated during the feed of the television from Goldstone.
We'll play that for you now.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Houston, this is Apollo 10.
Is that procedure still good for today?
When we disable the C and D jets, do we disable the C and D - the C roll jets, also?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Stand by.
That's affirmative, 10.
We want you to disable all jets on quads C and D.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Note that Duke is instructing the crew to disable SM RCS quads that are adjacent to each other.
They would normally use thrusters on opposite sides of the SM to maintain symmetrical firing.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Roger.
They're disabled.
transcript.speaker: Young

Charles Barry.

Berry a brief personal report.
Spacecraft Commander Stafford summarized by saying the general condition of the crew is excellent.
He added "I feel great.

We all feel just great." They were asked why they had each taken a Lomotil tablet, Stafford replied that all three had been troubled with stomach gas as a result of gas in the water and had taken the Lomotil tablet in an effort to relieve this condition.
Stafford added that the Lomotil seemed to help and was advised by Dr.
Berry that this was satisfactory.
Dr.
Berry suggested that the crew increase its consumption of water.
Stafford replied that he was aware that water consumption was down because of the gas in the water but that the crew members were trying to drink more fruit juices and eat more of the wet food packs.
Stafford added that he would try to increase the water intake.

Stafford said that he and the crew had been spending time reviewing upcoming activities, he noted that midcourse maneuver No.
4 would not be made, and requested that the crew's sleep period be extended.

Meanwhile, in a related subject to Apollo 10, more than 175 astronomers the world over will be focusing their telescopes on the lunar surface while the Apollo 10 crew spends the next two days orbiting the Moon.
The project is called Lunar International Observers Network.
Acronym is LION, and the purpose of the project is to determine whether ground observations of lunar events can be confirmed by the Apollo 10 crew.
LION is a coordinated program of the National Aeronautics and Space Administration with the Smithsonian Institute Center for the study of short lived phenomena in Cambridge, Massachusetts.
Participating astronomers are located in 34 different countries.
Lunar event sightings will be related through the Smithsonian Center to the Science Support Room here at the Manned Spacecraft Center, where scientists will evaluate the report.
